title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Casey has recently started trying to eat more healthily and has asked for your advice. Write an email to Casey giving tips on how to improve their diet, suggesting some easy healthy recipes, and explaining what changes you have made to your own eating habits.

Write your email to Casey.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Casey,

It's great to hear that you're trying to eat more healthily — it really does make such a difference to how you feel! Here are a few tips that have worked well for me.

First, try to plan your meals at the start of each week. This makes it much easier to avoid reaching for unhealthy snacks when you're tired and hungry. I always prepare a big batch of food on Sundays, which saves so much time.

For easy healthy recipes, I'd suggest starting with overnight oats for breakfast — just mix oats, milk, and fruit and leave them in the fridge overnight. For lunch, a big salad with chickpeas, roasted vegetables, and a tahini dressing is filling and delicious.

Personally, I cut out fizzy drinks six months ago and replaced them with water and herbal teas. That single change made me feel noticeably more energetic almost immediately.

Good luck — you've got this!

Take care,
Francesca

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
Your local council has announced plans to build a new fast-food restaurant near your school. Write a letter to the council expressing your concerns about the impact this could have on students' eating habits and suggesting healthier alternatives for the area.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Members of the Council,

I am writing to express my concerns regarding the recently announced plans to open a fast-food restaurant directly opposite Greendale Secondary School. While I understand the economic benefits this development may bring, I believe the potential harm to students' health warrants serious consideration.

Research clearly demonstrates that easy access to fast food leads to poorer dietary choices among young people. A restaurant of this type so close to a school will inevitably become a lunchtime destination, undermining the efforts of both parents and teachers to encourage healthy eating habits.

I would strongly encourage the council to consider alternative developments that might better serve the community and its young people. For example, a cafe or restaurant offering affordable, nutritious meals would be far more appropriate in this location. Alternatively, the space could be used for a weekly farmers' market, giving residents access to fresh, local produce.

I trust the council will take these concerns seriously.

Yours sincerely,
Harris Thompson

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your school health committee has asked you to write a report on students' eating habits at your school. Include findings about what students typically eat, why healthy eating can be difficult for students, and recommendations for promoting better nutrition.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Student Eating Habits at Elmwood College

Introduction
This report presents findings from a survey of 180 students regarding their daily eating habits, with the aim of informing a new school nutrition initiative.

Current Eating Habits
Results revealed that only 38% of students eat breakfast before school. At lunchtime, the majority choose sandwiches or hot meals from the canteen, but vending machine snacks — largely crisps and chocolate — are also very popular. In the evenings, many students rely on convenience foods due to time pressures from homework and activities.

Barriers to Healthy Eating
Students identified cost, lack of time, and limited cooking knowledge as the main obstacles. Several students noted that healthy options in the school canteen are less appealing than the alternatives.

Recommendations
The committee should consider introducing cooking workshops for students, expanding the range of affordable healthy options in the canteen, and replacing vending machine items with fruit, nuts, and wholegrain snacks. A school garden project could also connect students more meaningfully with where food comes from.
